module.title: Elbow MRI Orientation
module.subtitle: Protocols, planes, and sequences — and translating the clinical question into a focused, complete search
topics: Plane-to-structure map: axial = nerves/origins/biceps footprint, coronal = UCL/LCL/epicondyles/radiocapitellar, sagittal = fossae/triceps
FABS positioning: laying the distal biceps footprint flat for partial-vs-complete grading and retraction
MR arthrography vs non-contrast: the T-sign, loose bodies, and when contrast changes the operation
Sequences: fluid-sensitive fat-sat for edema/tears vs T1 for marrow/anatomy; confirm in ≥2 planes
Normal-variant pitfalls: the capitellar pseudodefect, transverse trochlear ridge, and distal-UCL synovial recess
learningObjectives: Assign each imaging plane (axial, coronal, sagittal, FABS) to the structures it best shows, and state why capitellar OCD must be read on coronal and sagittal together rather than sagittal alone

Choose the right protocol — routine non-contrast elbow, FABS positioning, or MR arthrography — and recognize the specific clinical questions (distal biceps grading, the UCL T-sign, loose bodies, OCD stability) that justify each
Match fluid-sensitive fat-sat and T1 sequences to the tissue question they answer, and confirm every finding in at least two planes

Translate the clinical question (UCL vs medial epicondylitis vs ulnar nerve vs OCD vs distal biceps) into a focused but complete search, and avoid over-calling the capitellar pseudodefect, transverse trochlear ridge, and the normal distal-UCL synovial recess

commonMistakes.mistake: Staging a capitellar OCD off the sagittal plane alone (or off a single plane)
commonMistakes.correction: Read capitellar OCD on coronal AND sagittal together — the coronal face shows the radiocapitellar contact zone where the lesion lives, while the sagittal defines its anterior-to-posterior extent and contour. Confirm any osteochondral finding in ≥2 planes before calling it, and remember the available stacks here are coronal T2-FS, so full OCD staging awaits sagittal acquisition.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reflexively ordering MR arthrography for every elbow, or treating an elbow 'ABER'/valgus-applied sequence as a validated routine like the shoulder
commonMistakes.correction: Non-contrast 3T MRI answers most elbow questions (tendons, OCD, nerves, occult fracture, loose bodies). Add direct MR arthrography specifically when a UCL undersurface (T-sign) tear is suspected but non-contrast is equivocal, or when loose-body count/OCD stability will change the operation. A valgus-applied or flexed positioning adjunct can open the medial joint, but the elbow evidence base is thin compared with the shoulder ABER — present it as an adjunct, not a routine sequence.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling the capitellar pseudodefect, the transverse trochlear ridge, or a small distal-UCL synovial recess a lesion
commonMistakes.correction: The pseudodefect of the capitellum is a normal posterolateral/postero-inferior bare-area groove at the capitellum–lateral-epicondyle junction — not an osteochondral lesion; the transverse trochlear ridge/groove is likewise a normal contour. A small synovial recess near the distal UCL is normal — the abnormal T-sign threshold is fluid tracking BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge, not any sliver of fluid at the attachment.

topicContent.content:
### Plane-to-Structure Map: Axial, Coronal, Sagittal

The first orientation skill is knowing **which plane answers which question** — because most elbow structures have one plane that displays them in profile and others that average through them.

| Plane | Best for | The clinical question it answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial** | Nerves (ulnar in the cubital tunnel, radial/PIN at the radiocapitellar level), common flexor/extensor origins in cross-section, the **annular ligament** around the radial neck, the distal biceps footprint | "Is there ulnar neuritis or flexion subluxation?" "Where is the entrapment?" |
| **Coronal** | The **UCL anterior bundle** to the sublime tubercle, the RCL/LUCL complex, the common flexor and extensor origins, and the **radiocapitellar contact zone** | "Is there a UCL tear (the T-sign), epicondylitis, or an LCL-origin tear?" |
| **Sagittal** | Olecranon/coronoid **fossae and the radiocapitellar recess** for loose bodies, the olecranon/triceps insertion, the anterior/posterior fat pads | "Is there a loose body or a triceps tear?" |

**Capitellar OCD is the key cross-plane rule:** read it on **coronal + sagittal together**, not sagittal alone. The coronal shows the lesion at the radiocapitellar surface where it lives; the sagittal defines its anterior-to-posterior extent and contour. A lesion convincing on one plane but absent on the orthogonal plane is more likely a partial-volume artifact than a real defect.

**Build note:** as of this build the only stacks on hand are **coronal T2-FS**, so UCL, epicondylitis, LCL-origin, and the coronal OCD face can be taught now; axial, sagittal, FABS, and arthrographic stacks are needed before image-driven nerve, biceps-footprint, loose-body, and full OCD-staging teaching.
topicContent.pearl: Each elbow plane owns a question: axial for nerves and the biceps footprint, coronal for the UCL and the radiocapitellar OCD face, sagittal for loose bodies and the triceps — and capitellar OCD must be read on coronal AND sagittal together, never sagittal alone.

topicContent.content:
### FABS Positioning: Profiling the Distal Biceps Footprint

The distal biceps tendon runs **obliquely** to its radial-tuberosity footprint, so on standard planes it is foreshortened and partial-volumed — you cannot reliably grade a partial tear or measure retraction. **FABS** solves this.

**FABS = Flexed elbow, ABducted shoulder, forearm Supinated**, with the patient **prone** and the arm overhead. This position lays the distal biceps tendon **flat and in-plane all the way to its radial-tuberosity footprint**, so you can:
- Distinguish **partial** (some fibers still reaching the tuberosity) from **complete** (full discontinuity) tears
- **Measure retraction** — where the stump sits, in cm
- Assess the **bifurcated footprint** (short head distal/anterior, long head proximal/posterior)

**Order FABS when the question is the distal biceps** — a middle-aged patient with a flexion-loading injury, antecubital ecchymosis, weak supination, or a positive hook test. The two management-changing variables FABS lets you report are **partial-vs-complete** and **retraction**, the features that drive repair urgency. (The lacertus-fibrosus status, the other key variable, is read on axial.)

**Pitfall to avoid:** FABS is for the **distal biceps**, not the UCL — don't confuse the two when selecting positioning.
topicContent.pearl: When the distal biceps is the question, add the FABS view (Flexed elbow, ABducted shoulder, Supinated forearm, prone) — it lays the tendon flat to its radial-tuberosity footprint so you can grade partial-vs-complete and measure retraction, which standard planes foreshorten.

topicContent.content:
### MR Arthrography vs Non-Contrast: When Contrast Changes the Operation

**Non-contrast 3T MRI is the default** and answers most elbow questions — tendons, OCD, nerves, occult fracture, loose bodies. **Direct MR arthrography** (intra-articular dilute gadolinium) earns its needle only when the added detail will change a decision.

**MR arthrography is the most sensitive study for:**
- The **partial undersurface UCL tear — the T-sign**: intra-articular contrast undercuts the deep fibers at the sublime tubercle, making an articular-sided partial tear conspicuous that non-contrast MRI undercalls. This is the classic throwing injury.
- **Intra-articular loose bodies and chondral delamination/flaps**: contrast outlines bodies and tracks under a delaminated flap.

**Add it when:**
- A thrower's exam says UCL but **non-contrast MRI is equivocal**
- **OCD stability** will change the surgical decision
- You need to **count loose bodies** pre-op (CT/CT arthrography is also strong for ossified bodies)

**The shoulder-ABER caveat (don't over-promise):** an abduction/valgus-applied or flexed positioning adjunct *can* open the medial joint to improve undersurface-tear conspicuity, but the **elbow evidence base is thin** compared with the shoulder ABER. Present it as an **adjunct, not a validated routine sequence**.

**Image-availability caveat for this course:** the available stacks are **non-arthrographic coronal T2-FS**, so the T-sign is taught here as a concept on coronal non-contrast images — with the explicit reminder that **MR arthrography is more sensitive** for it.
topicContent.pearl: Reach for MR arthrography only when it changes the operation — confirming the partial undersurface UCL T-sign, detecting/counting loose bodies, or settling OCD stability when non-contrast is equivocal; non-contrast 3T answers most elbow cases, and an elbow valgus/ABER adjunct is not a validated routine.

topicContent.content:
### Sequences: Fluid-Sensitive Fat-Sat vs T1 — and Confirming in ≥2 Planes

Match the **sequence to the tissue question**, exactly as in the other joints.

| Sequence | Reads | Use it for |
|---|---|---|
| **Fluid-sensitive fat-sat** (PD/T2 FS or **STIR**) | **Fluid + edema** | Tendon/ligament tears, the **OCD fluid rim**, marrow injury/stress, effusions, and **subacute muscle denervation edema** |
| **T1** | **Marrow, fat, anatomy** | Marrow **replacement** (confluent low-T1 below muscle = red flag), arthrogram contrast, occult fracture lines, chronic **fatty muscle atrophy** |

Think **T1 = anatomy/marrow, fluid-sensitive = pathology/edema.** A fracture line is a low-signal line on T1 with **fat-suppressed-bright** marrow edema around it; a bone *bruise* shows edema **without** a line.

**The orientation discipline: confirm every finding in at least two planes.** A signal abnormality on one plane that disappears on the orthogonal plane is usually **partial-volume averaging or a normal contour**, not pathology. This is why the OCD rule (coronal + sagittal) and the LUCL rule (coronal + axial) exist — partial-volume averaging is the single most common reason a real lesion is missed *or* a normal variant is over-called.

**Available here:** coronal **T2-FS** — the workhorse fluid-sensitive plane for the UCL, the T-sign concept, epicondylitis, and the LCL origin.
topicContent.pearl: Fluid-sensitive fat-sat shows the pathology (tears, edema, the OCD rim) and T1 shows the anatomy and the marrow red flag — and a finding seen on only one plane is partial-volume artifact until you confirm it on a second.

topicContent.content:
### Normal-Variant Pitfalls — Don't Call Them Lesions

Three normal findings are routinely over-called at the elbow. Recognizing them is part of orientation, because each lives exactly where a real lesion would and each sends a normal elbow toward unnecessary imaging or referral.

| Variant | Location | How to call it normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Pseudodefect of the capitellum** | **Posterolateral / postero-inferior** bare-area groove at the capitellum–lateral-epicondyle junction | A normal contour notch where the articular cartilage ends posteriorly — **not** an osteochondral lesion. True capitellar OCD is **anterolateral/central** (the radiocapitellar contact zone), with a fluid rim, cyst, or fragment |
| **Transverse trochlear ridge / trochlear groove** | Trochlea | A normal bony ridge/groove in the trochlear contour — not an osteochondral defect |
| **Normal distal-UCL synovial recess** | Just distal to the UCL attachment at the sublime tubercle | A small synovial recess can normally exist; the abnormal **T-sign** threshold is fluid/contrast tracking **beyond the articular-cartilage edge**, not a couple of millimeters undercutting the most distal fibers |

**The location lesson:** for capitellar OCD, the real lesion is **anterolateral/central** and the mimic (pseudodefect) is **posterolateral** — location separates variant from pathology. For the UCL, a normal recess sits at the attachment but a true partial tear has contrast extending **past the cartilage margin**. Anchor each call to **location + a corroborating sign** (fluid rim, cyst, fragment, contrast beyond the cartilage edge), not to signal alone.
topicContent.pearl: The capitellar pseudodefect is a posterolateral bare-area groove — true OCD is anterolateral/central — and a small distal-UCL synovial recess is normal unless fluid tracks beyond the articular-cartilage edge; location plus a corroborating sign, not signal alone, separates variant from lesion.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 14-year-old gymnast with lateral elbow pain and catching has a radiograph showing a capitellar lucency. To assess the osteochondral lesion and judge its stability, which approach best fits how capitellar OCD should be read on MRI?

options.text: Read it on the sagittal plane alone, since the sagittal best profiles the capitellar cartilage
Read it on coronal and sagittal planes together, confirming the lesion in at least two planes
Read it on the axial plane, which best shows the radiocapitellar joint
Read it on a single fluid-sensitive coronal image, since one fluid-sensitive plane is sufficient

moduleQuiz.explanation: Capitellar OCD must be read on coronal AND sagittal together — the coronal shows the lesion at the radiocapitellar contact zone where it lives, while the sagittal defines its anterior-to-posterior extent and contour. Reading any single plane (sagittal alone, coronal alone, or axial) risks both missing the lesion and over-calling a partial-volume artifact, which is why confirming every osteochondral finding in at least two planes is the orientation rule. The axial plane is for nerves, origins, and the annular ligament, not for the capitellar OCD face.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 47-year-old man felt a tearing sensation lifting a heavy box with the elbow flexed and now has antecubital ecchymosis and weak supination. On standard planes the distal biceps tendon is foreshortened and hard to grade. Which positioning best lays the tendon in-plane to its footprint so you can grade a partial-vs-complete tear and measure retraction?

options.text: A valgus-applied (elbow 'ABER') positioning adjunct, the validated routine for tendon grading
MR arthrography, because intra-articular contrast is required to see the footprint
FABS — flexed elbow, abducted shoulder, supinated forearm, prone
Standard axial imaging alone, which already profiles the bicipitoradial footprint

moduleQuiz.explanation: FABS (Flexed elbow, ABducted shoulder, Supinated forearm, prone) lays the obliquely-running distal biceps tendon flat and in-plane all the way to its radial-tuberosity footprint, allowing you to distinguish partial from complete tears and measure retraction — the features that drive repair urgency. A valgus-applied/'ABER' adjunct is for opening the medial joint to improve UCL undersurface-tear conspicuity and is not a validated routine for the biceps. MR arthrography is not required to grade the biceps footprint, and standard axial imaging foreshortens the tendon (axial is, however, where you read the lacertus fibrosus).

moduleQuiz.stem: A 19-year-old pitcher has medial elbow pain and a positive moving-valgus stress test; non-contrast 3T MRI is equivocal for a partial undersurface UCL tear at the sublime tubercle. Which statement best reflects when to add MR arthrography?

options.text: Add MR arthrography here, because it is the most sensitive study for the partial undersurface UCL (T-sign) tear when non-contrast imaging is equivocal
MR arthrography should be ordered routinely on every elbow MRI for completeness
Avoid MR arthrography; an elbow 'ABER' sequence is a validated routine that replaces it
Avoid MR arthrography; non-contrast MRI is equally sensitive for the T-sign, so contrast never changes management

moduleQuiz.explanation: Direct MR arthrography is the most sensitive study for the partial undersurface UCL tear (the T-sign), because intra-articular contrast undercuts the deep fibers at the sublime tubercle; it is the right addition when a thrower's exam says UCL but non-contrast MRI is equivocal (and likewise for loose-body detection/counting or when OCD stability will change the operation). It is not a routine for every elbow — non-contrast 3T answers most cases. An elbow valgus-applied/'ABER' adjunct can help open the medial joint but is NOT a validated routine like the shoulder ABER, and non-contrast MRI is genuinely less sensitive than arthrography for the T-sign, so contrast can indeed change management.

module.title: Elbow Search Pattern
module.subtitle: A repeatable bones-first read that defeats satisfaction-of-search across the thrower's, the tennis, and the post-trauma elbow
topics: Why a fixed, bones-first order beats lesion-spotting
The eight-step elbow search pattern
Satisfaction-of-search traps: the epicondylitis that hides a ligament
Linked-structure habits: the medial valgus triad and the post-trauma O'Driscoll circle
Sweeping for loose bodies and the plica mimic, then writing a management-grade impression
learningObjectives: Apply a reproducible eight-step elbow MRI search pattern that reads context and bones/marrow before ligaments, tendons, and nerves

Explain how satisfaction-of-search produces the classic elbow misses (a deep common-extensor/LUCL tear behind lateral epicondylitis; the UCL and ulnar nerve behind medial epicondylitis) and adopt a habit that defeats it

Read medial pain as the valgus triad (UCL + flexor-pronator + ulnar nerve) and the post-dislocation elbow as the O'Driscoll circle (LCL/LUCL, coronoid including the anteromedial facet, radial head, Osborne-Cotterill, heterotopic ossification)
Produce a structured impression that names the structure, the grade/severity, and the management consequence

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling lateral epicondylitis (or medial epicondylitis) and stopping the read
commonMistakes.correction: The symptomatic tendinosis is rarely the management-changing lesion. Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest beneath every common-extensor tendinosis (a deep undersurface tear there means PLRI), and trace the UCL to the sublime tubercle plus interrogate the ulnar nerve beneath every common-flexor tendinosis. Finish all eight steps every time.

commonMistakes.mistake: Dismissing a small coronoid fragment in a post-dislocation elbow because it looks small on radiographs
commonMistakes.correction: Small does not mean stable at the coronoid. An anteromedial-facet fracture is highly unstable, implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability, and is surgical; untreated it drives early arthrosis. In any dislocation/terrible-triad elbow, close the O'Driscoll circle — LCL/LUCL, coronoid, radial head, Osborne-Cotterill impaction, and heterotopic ossification.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reading tendons and ligaments first and leaving bones/marrow for the end
commonMistakes.correction: Read context and bones/marrow as steps 1-2 while attention is freshest. The high-consequence osseous lesions (unstable OCD, olecranon stress fracture, occult radial-head fracture, the anteromedial coronoid facet, a marrow-replacing lesion) are exactly what gets lost to fatigue and satisfaction-of-search once an obvious tendinopathy is found.

topicContent.content:
### Why a Fixed, Bones-First Order Beats Lesion-Spotting

The natural instinct is to **chase the obvious finding** — the thickened, bright common-extensor origin in a tennis player, the fluid-filled olecranon bursa — and then quit. That is **satisfaction of search**, and at the elbow it is precisely how the management-changing lesion gets missed: the eye that locks onto lateral epicondylitis glides right past the **deep common-extensor/LUCL tear** sitting underneath it, and the elbow that should have gone to a ligament surgeon goes to rehab instead.

A reproducible order forces you to **visit every compartment regardless of where your attention is pulled**, so the second — often more dangerous — finding still gets seen.

**Why it works:**
- **Coverage, not recall** — you check a structure because it is next on the list, not because you happened to remember it.
- **Calibrates attention** — the high-consequence compartment (**bones/marrow**) is read *early*, while attention is freshest, not last when fatigue sets in.
- **Auditable dictation** — the report writes itself in a fixed sequence reviewers can follow.

**The elbow caveat on planes:** as of this build the only stacks on hand are **coronal T2-FS**, which carries the UCL, the LCL/LUCL origin, the common flexor/extensor origins, and the coronal face of a capitellar OCD. A complete search still *names* the structures that need **axial** (nerves, annular ligament, biceps footprint), **sagittal** (fossae/loose bodies, OCD's anteroposterior extent), and **FABS** (distal biceps) — so you can flag what the available planes cannot answer rather than silently skip it.

**Management impact:** lesion-spotting over-calls the symptomatic structure and under-calls the silent surgical one. A fixed order keeps you from discharging an elbow to a counterforce brace when the marrow held an unstable OCD or the LUCL was torn.
topicContent.pearl: The first finding you see is rarely the one that changes disposition — a fixed, bones-first order exists precisely to make you keep looking after you have already found something.

topicContent.content:
### The Eight-Step Elbow Search Pattern

Drive every study through the same sequence. **Context and bones/marrow go first** (steps 1-2), while attention is freshest, because that is where the disposition-changing lesions hide. **Confirm every finding in ≥2 planes** and pair **T1** (marrow, fat, occult fracture lines, arthrogram contrast) with **fluid-sensitive fat-sat** (PD/T2 FS or STIR for edema, fluid, tears, denervation).

| Step | Region | High-yield target |
|---|---|---|
| **1 Verify** | Protocol & question | Side, non-contrast vs arthrogram, FABS present?; planes/sequences on hand; map the symptom (medial/lateral/anterior/posterior/mechanical) |
| **2 Bones/Marrow** | Capitellum, radial head, trochlea, olecranon, coronoid | **Unstable OCD vs Panner**, olecranon stress line, occult radial-head fracture, CRITOE, radiocapitellar line, marrow replacement |
| **3 Medial ligament** | UCL anterior bundle | Sublime-tubercle attachment, **T-sign** (fluid beyond the cartilage edge), proximal/mid/distal, chronic spur |
| **4 Lateral ligament** | LCL/LUCL, RCL, annular | **LUCL to the supinator crest**, undersurface tear deep to common extensor, **anteromedial coronoid facet**, PLRI signs |
| **5 Tendons** | Biceps, triceps, flexor/extensor origins | Biceps partial-vs-complete + lacertus, triceps % width, epicondylitis grade |
| **6 Nerves** | Ulnar, median/AIN, radial/PIN | Caliber/signal vs contralateral & median internal control, flexion subluxation, denervation pattern |
| **7 Joint/Synovium** | Cartilage, effusion, bursa, recesses | Loose bodies in dependent recesses, radiocapitellar plica, septic vs aseptic bursa |
| **8 Review/Red flags** | Masses + final management | Confluent low-T1 replacement, confirm the urgent injury, state the single disposition |

**Bones at step 2, not last** — the unstable OCD, the posteromedial olecranon stress fracture, and the occult radial-head fracture are the findings that change today's plan.
topicContent.pearl: Run bones/marrow as step 2, not last: the unstable OCD, the olecranon stress fracture, and the occult radial-head fracture are disposition-changers that get missed to end-of-read fatigue.

topicContent.content:
### Satisfaction-of-Search Traps: The Epicondylitis That Hides a Ligament

Two over-learned diagnoses — lateral and medial epicondylitis — are the elbow's most reliable satisfaction-of-search bait. Each sits **directly superficial to a ligament whose injury is the surgical problem**, so calling the tendinosis and stopping is how the operative lesion gets buried.

**Lateral pain → don't stop at the common extensor.** The **RCL/LUCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon**, so a **deep undersurface tear** there hides beneath what looks like simple "tennis elbow." A deep/full-thickness common-extensor tear with a **fluid-bright gap that undercuts or communicates with the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies PLRI** — a surgical problem and a classic complication of prior **lateral steroid injection or release**. Trace the LUCL to the **supinator crest** before signing off any high-grade lateral epicondylitis. Conversely, do **not** over-call reactive lateral-epicondyle marrow edema or a thin RCL signal as an LCL tear.

**Medial pain → don't stop at the common flexor.** The **UCL** runs deep to the flexor-pronator origin, and the **ulnar nerve sits immediately posterior** in the cubital tunnel. Medial epicondylitis and UCL injury frequently coexist, especially in throwers, and a concomitant UCL tear shifts a thrower's conversation toward reconstruction.

| You see | You must also read | Because |
|---|---|---|
| Common-extensor tendinosis | **LUCL to the supinator crest** | Deep undersurface tear → PLRI (surgical) |
| Common-flexor tendinosis | **UCL to the sublime tubercle + ulnar nerve** | Coexistent UCL tear / ulnar neuritis changes the plan |

**Defeat tactic:** make the deep ligament a *required* next step the moment you call an epicondylitis — it is on the list, not a matter of memory.
topicContent.pearl: Read lateral epicondylitis? Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest. Read medial epicondylitis? Trace the UCL and check the ulnar nerve — the adjacent ligament is where the surgery hides.

topicContent.content:
### Linked-Structure Habits: The Medial Valgus Triad and the Post-Trauma O'Driscoll Circle

Two elbow situations injure structures **as a group**, so reading them as a linked set — not one at a time — is the habit that prevents the second miss.

**The medial valgus triad (every thrower's medial-pain read).** Valgus overload injures three structures together, so read them together:

| Triad member | What to document |
|---|---|
| **UCL anterior bundle** | Sublime-tubercle attachment, T-sign, chronic traction spur |
| **Flexor-pronator origin** | Tendinosis vs partial/full-thickness tear |
| **Ulnar nerve** | Caliber/signal vs contralateral & median; flexion subluxation |

Miss any one and you under-call the injury; the **sublime-tubercle traction spur** is a common bony clue to chronic valgus overload.

**The post-trauma O'Driscoll circle (every dislocation/terrible-triad elbow).** Soft-tissue disruption proceeds **lateral-to-medial** (**LCL/LUCL first → capsule → finally the MCL**), which is why the **LCL is the primary lesion in most simple dislocations** and valgus stability is often preserved. Close the circle in order:

1. **LCL/LUCL** — the usual primary lesion; comment on LUCL integrity explicitly.
2. **Coronoid** — including the **anteromedial facet**, which looks small on radiographs but implies **varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI)** and is surgical; **small does not mean stable**. (Regan-Morrey grades coronoid *height*; O'Driscoll classifies the *anteromedial facet*.)
3. **Radial head** — the third leg of the **terrible triad** (dislocation + radial-head fracture + coronoid fracture).
4. **Osborne-Cotterill** posterolateral capitellar impaction + posterolateral capsular avulsion — the lateral analog of a Hill-Sachs that perpetuates recurrent PLRI.
5. **Heterotopic ossification** — a common, function-limiting cause of the stiff post-traumatic elbow.

**Management impact:** the coronoid and the LCL are the reconstructive keystones of the terrible triad, and an unflagged anteromedial-facet fracture is the classic don't-miss that drives early arthrosis.
topicContent.pearl: Medial pain is a triad (UCL + flexor-pronator + ulnar nerve); the dislocated elbow is a circle (LCL/LUCL → coronoid/anteromedial facet → radial head → Osborne-Cotterill → HO) — read the group, not one structure.

topicContent.content:
### Sweeping for Loose Bodies, the Plica Mimic, and Writing a Management-Grade Impression

**The mechanical-symptom sweep.** Any locking/catching history earns a deliberate pass through the three **dependent recesses** where loose bodies settle — the **coronoid fossa, the olecranon fossa, and the radiocapitellar recess** — best seen against bright joint fluid on sagittal fluid-sensitive images (GRE blooms ossified bodies; CT/CT arthrography is most sensitive for counting small ossified bodies pre-op). Don't ascribe every loose body to a throwing lesion: **primary elbow OA** (coronoid/olecranon-tip and fossa osteophytes, chondral loss) is the most common source in the non-thrower/laborer.

**The plica mimic.** A thickened **radiocapitellar plica / posterolateral synovial fold** (pathologic beyond **~3 mm** with mechanical symptoms) causes lateral catching and mimics a loose body or OCD — check for it before you commit.

**Write a management-grade impression.** The read ends in **structured language** that names the structure, the grade, and the consequence — not an anatomic list:

| Axis | Vocabulary |
|---|---|
| Grade | low-grade vs high-grade; partial undersurface vs full-thickness |
| Acuity | acute vs chronic; attenuated vs avulsed |
| Stability | stable vs instability-pattern (PLRI, VPMRI) |
| Nerve | ulnar subluxation / edema / denervation pattern |
| Fragment | displacement in **mm**; intra-articular vs not |

Then **prioritize the single management-changing finding** — rehab/injection, surgical referral, or urgent escalation — rather than burying it. A confluent **low-T1 marrow-replacing** lesion (signal below muscle/disc), a complete biceps/triceps/UCL/LUCL avulsion, an unstable OCD, or an anteromedial coronoid facet fracture each outranks an incidental degenerative finding and leads the impression.
topicContent.pearl: End every read with a one-line disposition that names the single management-changing finding in structured terms (grade, acuity, stability, mm) — an anatomic list buries the lesion that actually changes the plan.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 45-year-old recreational tennis player has chronic lateral elbow pain that has failed rehab. Coronal T2-FS MRI shows a thickened, hyperintense common extensor origin. When you apply the elbow search pattern, which adjacent structure must you specifically interrogate before signing off, because a tear there would change management from debridement to ligament surgery?

options.text: The anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament at the sublime tubercle
The lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL) traced to the supinator crest
The distal biceps footprint on the radial tuberosity
The annular ligament around the radial neck

moduleQuiz.explanation: The RCL/LUCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon, so a deep undersurface tear hides beneath what looks like simple lateral epicondylitis. A deep/full-thickness common-extensor tear with a fluid-bright gap that undercuts or communicates with the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI) — a surgical problem and a classic complication of prior lateral steroid injection or release. Tracing the LUCL to the supinator crest is the required next step that defeats this satisfaction-of-search trap. The UCL/sublime tubercle is the partner of MEDIAL epicondylitis, not lateral.

moduleQuiz.stem: An MRI/CT of a post-dislocation elbow shows a small anteromedial coronoid facet fracture fragment. Why does the search pattern require you to flag this rather than dismiss it as incidental because it looks small on radiographs?

options.text: A small fragment is reliably stable and can be managed with early motion
It indicates valgus instability and a torn UCL anterior bundle
It implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability and is surgical; untreated it drives early arthrosis
It confirms a coexisting distal biceps avulsion that needs urgent repair

moduleQuiz.explanation: At the coronoid, small does not mean stable. The anteromedial-facet fracture looks small on radiographs but is highly unstable, implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI) — a distinct mechanism from valgus and from PLRI — and is surgical; untreated VPMRI drives early arthrosis. In any dislocation/terrible-triad elbow, the search pattern closes the O'Driscoll circle (LCL/LUCL, coronoid including the anteromedial facet, radial head, Osborne-Cotterill impaction, heterotopic ossification), and the coronoid and LCL are the reconstructive keystones.

moduleQuiz.stem: Which sequencing principle most directly explains why the eight-step elbow search pattern places bones and marrow at step 2 rather than at the end of the read?

options.text: Bone findings are usually incidental, so reading them early gets them out of the way
The high-consequence osseous lesions are vulnerable to satisfaction-of-search and end-of-read fatigue after an obvious tendinopathy
Marrow can only be assessed on the first sequence acquired in the protocol
Ligament and tendon findings must always be confirmed before any bone finding is reported

moduleQuiz.explanation: A fixed order calibrates attention so the high-consequence compartment (bones/marrow) is read while attention is freshest. The unstable OCD, the posteromedial olecranon stress fracture, the occult radial-head fracture, the anteromedial coronoid facet, and a confluent low-T1 marrow-replacing lesion are exactly the disposition-changing findings that get lost to satisfaction-of-search and end-of-read fatigue once an obvious tendinopathy has captured the eye. These lesions are not incidental (ruling out A), and marrow is assessed across T1 and fluid-sensitive sequences in every plane (ruling out C).

module.title: Bones, Marrow & Osteochondral
module.subtitle: Capitellar OCD vs Panner, the thrower's stress lesions, CRITOE and the radiocapitellar line, occult fracture, and loose bodies
topics: Capitellar OCD vs Panner disease: age, focality, and reversibility
OCD stability criteria: the fluid-undercutting line, cysts, and cartilage breach
The thrower's stress lesions: Little League elbow, olecranon stress, and valgus extension overload
CRITOE, the radiocapitellar line, and the occult radial-head fracture (fat-pad sign)
Loose bodies, primary OA, and the high-stakes pediatric misses
learningObjectives: Distinguish capitellar osteochondritis dissecans from Panner disease by age, focality, and reversibility, and judge OCD stability from the interface signs read on coronal plus sagittal

Recognize the thrower's osseous stress lesions — medial epicondyle apophysis (Little League elbow), olecranon stress reaction vs fracture, and the posteromedial olecranon osteophyte of valgus extension overload
Apply CRITOE and the radiocapitellar line, work up the displaced posterior fat-pad sign as an occult radial-head fracture, and localize loose bodies
Identify the high-stakes pediatric misses — an intra-articularly incarcerated medial epicondyle avulsion and the non-union-prone lateral condyle (Salter-Harris IV) fracture

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a capitellar OCD unstable on the basis of surrounding marrow edema, or applying a hard 5 mm subchondral-cyst cutoff
commonMistakes.correction: Surrounding marrow edema is present in stable AND unstable lesions and is not a stability sign; an isolated high-T2 line without a cartilage breach or cyst is INDETERMINATE (it may be healing granulation tissue). Instability is a fluid-bright line completely undercutting the fragment (matching joint-fluid signal), a sizeable or multiple subchondral cyst(s), a cartilage cleft reaching the fragment, or a displaced fragment. The 5 mm cyst figure is De Smet's knee-derived criterion, not validated for the capitellum — teach 'sizeable or multiple cysts,' not a hard millimeter cutoff.

commonMistakes.mistake: Treating a displaced posterior fat pad with a 'normal' radiograph as no fracture, or sending every Mason II radial-head fracture to surgery on the 2 mm number
commonMistakes.correction: A visible (displaced) posterior fat pad in an adult after trauma means hemarthrosis from an occult intra-articular fracture — radial head most common — until proven otherwise; MRI shows the low-signal line plus fat-suppressed-bright marrow edema and separates a true fracture from a bone bruise (edema only). The surgical trigger is a mechanical BLOCK to forearm rotation, an articular fragment displaced enough to impede motion, or comminution (Mason III), not the 2 mm displacement alone — many Mason II without a block are managed nonoperatively.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ascribing every elbow loose body to a throwing lesion and clearing a child's 'fat pad without a line'
commonMistakes.correction: Primary elbow OA (coronoid/olecranon-tip and fossa osteophytes, capitellar/radial-head chondral loss) is the most common source of loose bodies and mechanical symptoms in the non-thrower/laborer. In a child, the lateral condyle fracture (Salter-Harris IV) is the most non-union/malunion-prone elbow fracture and is classically the subtle 'fat pad without an obvious line' that needs MRI or follow-up rather than reassurance.

topicContent.content:
### Capitellar OCD vs Panner Disease

Both are osteochondral lesions of the **capitellum** in young throwers and gymnasts, and both can present with lateral elbow pain and a flexion contracture — but they are different diseases with opposite trajectories. **Three discriminators settle it: age, focality, and reversibility.**

**Panner disease** — a self-limited **osteochondrosis/AVN of the ENTIRE capitellar ossific nucleus** in a younger child (**~5–10 yr**). The whole nucleus looks fragmented and low-signal, but there is **no discrete fragment, no subchondral cyst, and no loose body**. It is **reversible** and managed conservatively — the nucleus remodels and heals.

**Capitellar OCD** — a **FOCAL anterolateral/central osteochondral lesion** of the **adolescent/teen (~12–17 yr)** thrower or gymnast. Unlike Panner, an OCD lesion **can fragment, delaminate, and shed a loose body**, and it does not reliably heal once unstable.

| Feature | Panner | Capitellar OCD |
|---|---|---|
| Age | ~5–10 yr | ~12–17 yr |
| Extent | **Whole** ossific nucleus | **Focal** anterolateral/central |
| Fragment/cyst/loose body | No | Possible |
| Course | Self-limited, reversible | Can fragment; may need surgery |

**Read the lesion on coronal AND sagittal together** — the coronal shows the radiocapitellar contact face, the sagittal shows the anterior-to-posterior extent and contour. And do not call Panner or OCD on a **normal CRITOE secondary ossification center**, which is fragmented-looking by nature.
topicContent.pearl: Panner is the whole young capitellum (~5–10 yr) that heals; OCD is the focal teenage capitellum (~12–17 yr) that can fragment and shed a loose body — age plus focality plus the presence of a fragment/cyst separates them.

topicContent.content:
### OCD Stability Criteria — the Interface Is Everything

Once you have called a capitellar OCD, the question that changes management is **stable vs unstable**: a stable lesion is rested and followed; an unstable lesion stops throwing and goes to surgery (fixation, microfracture, fragment removal ± grafting). Stability is judged **at the fragment–bone interface**, read on **coronal + sagittal**.

**Unstable — ANY of:**
- A **T2 fluid-bright line completely undercutting the fragment**, matching joint-fluid signal (the most reliable adult criterion)
- A **sizeable or multiple subchondral cyst(s)** deep to the fragment
- A **high-signal cartilage cleft reaching the fragment** (delamination)
- A **displaced fragment or a loose body**

**Stable:** no surrounding fluid-bright signal, intact overlying cartilage, an in-situ fragment, and a low-signal demarcating margin.

| Sign | What it means |
|---|---|
| Fluid line fully undercutting fragment | **Unstable** |
| Sizeable/multiple subchondral cyst | **Unstable** |
| Cartilage cleft to the fragment | **Unstable** (delamination) |
| Displaced fragment / loose body | **Unstable** |
| **Surrounding marrow edema alone** | **NOT a stability sign** (stable and unstable both have it) |
| **Isolated high-T2 line, no cyst/breach** | **INDETERMINATE** (may be healing granulation) |

**Hedges to teach honestly:** the **5 mm cyst** figure is **De Smet's KNEE-derived criterion** (Dipaola is a separate staging system), **not validated for the capitellum** — say "sizeable or multiple cysts," not a hard cutoff. And the often-quoted **~0.92 sensitivity / 0.85 specificity** for MRI stability criteria comes from an **OCD-wide (predominantly knee) meta-analysis extrapolated to the elbow** — cite it that way or teach the signs qualitatively. When stability is indeterminate and will change the operation, **MR or CT arthrography** resolves it.
topicContent.pearl: OCD instability lives at the interface — a fluid line undercutting the fragment, a sizeable or multiple cyst, a cartilage breach, or a displaced fragment; surrounding edema alone is NOT instability, and an isolated high-T2 line is indeterminate.

topicContent.content:
### The Thrower's Osseous Stress Lesions

Repetitive valgus loads the medial column in tension and the posterior column in extension. Three osseous stress patterns recur, and each is graded by the **presence or absence of a discrete fracture line**.

**Little League elbow (medial epicondyle apophysitis/avulsion)** — in the skeletally immature thrower, repetitive valgus **traction** injures the medial epicondyle apophysis: a **widened, fluid-bright physis with adjacent marrow edema**. Frank avulsion needs the exact displacement reported. **Operative triggers in a throwing athlete: displacement ≥ 5 mm, an intra-articular/incarcerated fragment, or valgus instability** — report the millimeters and whether the fragment is intra-articular. (The mm cutoff is debated in the general population; ≥ 5 mm is the widely taught throwing-athlete trigger, and incarceration or instability mandates surgery regardless of mm.)

**Olecranon stress** — a discrete **low-signal fracture LINE** (classically posteromedial/mid-olecranon) plus marrow edema = **stress fracture** (longer rest ± fixation, especially if widening/displaced); **edema WITHOUT a line = lower-grade stress reaction**. The **olecranon apophysis is among the last to fuse** — roughly **mid-to-late teens (~15–17 yr, later in throwers)** — so a persistently widened, fluid-bright olecranon physis is a physeal stress injury; **compare to the contralateral side** rather than trusting a hard fusion age.

**Valgus extension overload (VEO)** — the chronic end of the chain: UCL insufficiency lets the **posteromedial olecranon** impinge in the olecranon fossa, producing a **posteromedial olecranon osteophyte** and a posterior-compartment loose body. The osteophyte causes the catching, but the **underlying UCL must be assessed** — debriding the osteophyte without addressing valgus laxity can unmask instability.

| Lesion | Imaging | Management hinge |
|---|---|---|
| Little League elbow | Widened, bright physis + edema | ≥ 5 mm / intra-articular / unstable → surgery |
| Olecranon stress | Line = fracture; edema only = reaction | Line → longer rest ± fixation |
| VEO | Posteromedial osteophyte + loose body | Assess the UCL before debriding |
topicContent.pearl: Edema is the smoke — find the fire: a discrete low-signal line makes an olecranon stress FRACTURE (rest ± fixation) versus edema-only stress reaction, and a posteromedial olecranon osteophyte should always send you back to interrogate the UCL.

topicContent.content:
### CRITOE, the Radiocapitellar Line, and the Occult Radial-Head Fracture

Three habits keep the bone read out of trouble: know the ossification order, check radial alignment, and respect the fat-pad sign.

**CRITOE** — the elbow's ossification sequence (approximate ages): **C**apitellum ~1, **R**adial head ~3, **I**nternal/medial epicondyle ~5, **T**rochlea ~7, **O**lecranon ~9, **E**xternal/lateral epicondyle ~11. The load-bearing rule: **the trochlea NEVER ossifies before the medial epicondyle.** If you 'see' the trochlea but **not** the medial epicondyle, the medial epicondyle has been **avulsed** — often into the joint.

**Radiocapitellar line** — a line through the **central long axis of the radial neck must bisect the capitellum on EVERY projection and plane**. Failure = radial head subluxation/dislocation, including an **occult Monteggia**. Repetitive valgus also drives radiocapitellar (lateral-compartment) osteochondral overload — the lateral partner of medial UCL injury.

**Occult radial-head fracture / fat-pad sign** — any **visible (displaced) POSTERIOR fat pad** in an adult after trauma means **hemarthrosis = occult intra-articular fracture** (radial head most common in adults; supracondylar in children) until proven otherwise. The anterior fat pad is normally seen; elevation is the **sail sign**. MRI shows a **low-signal line + fat-suppressed-bright marrow edema** and distinguishes a true fracture from a **bone bruise (edema only)**.

| Mason | Description |
|---|---|
| I | Non-/minimally displaced |
| II | Displaced (commonly > 2 mm) partial articular |
| III | Comminuted whole-head |

**Surgical trigger** is a **mechanical BLOCK to forearm rotation**, an articular fragment displaced enough to impede motion, or **comminution (Mason III)** — **NOT the 2 mm number alone**; many Mason II without a block are managed nonoperatively.
topicContent.pearl: A displaced posterior fat pad after adult elbow trauma is a fracture until disproven — radial head first — and the radial-head surgical trigger is a mechanical block to forearm rotation, not the 2 mm displacement by itself.

topicContent.content:
### Loose Bodies, Primary OA, and the High-Stakes Pediatric Misses

**Loose bodies** migrate to **dependent recesses** — the **coronoid fossa, olecranon fossa, and radiocapitellar recess** — and are best seen **against bright joint fluid on sagittal fluid-sensitive images**. **GRE blooms ossified bodies**, and **CT/CT arthrography is most sensitive** for small ossified bodies and for **counting them pre-operatively**. Always sweep the fossae in any locking/catching history, and check the **radiocapitellar plica (posterolateral synovial fold, pathologic when thickened beyond ~3 mm)** as a mimic.

**Don't ascribe every loose body to a throwing lesion.** **Primary elbow OA** — coronoid- and olecranon-tip osteophytes, fossa osteophytes, and capitellar/radial-head chondral loss — is the **most common source of loose bodies and mechanical symptoms in the non-thrower/laborer**.

**The high-stakes pediatric misses:**

| Miss | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| **Incarcerated medial epicondyle avulsion** | After a transient dislocation that **spontaneously reduced**, the avulsed medial epicondyle can be **trapped intra-articularly** — use CRITOE (trochlea visible without medial epicondyle → it's avulsed, often into the joint) |
| **Lateral condyle fracture (Salter-Harris IV)** | The elbow fracture **most prone to non-union/malunion**; often the subtle **'fat pad without an obvious line'** that needs MRI/follow-up, not reassurance |

Both pediatric lesions are the kind that look unimpressive on the first pass and declare themselves later — which is exactly why bones and marrow are **search-pattern step 2**, read while attention is freshest.
topicContent.pearl: Most non-thrower loose bodies come from primary elbow OA, not a throwing lesion — and in a child, a 'fat pad without a line' is a lateral condyle (Salter-Harris IV) fracture or an incarcerated medial epicondyle until proven otherwise.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 14-year-old competitive gymnast has lateral elbow pain and catching. MRI shows a focal anterolateral capitellar osteochondral lesion. Which single finding most reliably indicates that the lesion is UNSTABLE and should prompt surgical referral?

options.text: Surrounding capitellar marrow edema on fluid-sensitive images
A T2 fluid-bright line, matching joint-fluid signal, completely undercutting the fragment at the interface
Involvement of the entire capitellar ossific nucleus
An isolated high-T2 line at the interface with intact overlying cartilage and no subchondral cyst

moduleQuiz.explanation: A T2 fluid-bright line that completely undercuts the fragment and matches joint-fluid signal is the most reliable adult criterion for an unstable osteochondral fragment; a sizeable/multiple subchondral cyst, a cartilage cleft reaching the fragment, or a displaced fragment/loose body are the other instability signs. Surrounding marrow edema (A) is present in both stable and unstable lesions and is NOT a stability sign. Whole-nucleus involvement (C) describes Panner disease in a younger child, not a focal adolescent OCD. An isolated high-T2 line without a cyst or cartilage breach (D) is INDETERMINATE — it can be healing granulation tissue — and warrants MR/CT arthrography rather than an automatic 'unstable' call.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 30-year-old falls on an outstretched hand and has lateral elbow pain. Radiographs show a displaced posterior fat pad but no visible fracture line. What is the most appropriate interpretation and management driver?

options.text: The normal radiograph excludes fracture; the displaced posterior fat pad is a normal finding

An occult intra-articular fracture (radial head most likely) is present until proven otherwise; the surgical trigger is a mechanical block to forearm rotation or comminution, not 2 mm of displacement alone
Any radial-head fracture displaced 2 mm or more (Mason II) requires operative fixation regardless of motion
The posterolateral pseudodefect of the capitellum accounts for the finding and needs no further work-up

moduleQuiz.explanation: A visible (displaced) posterior fat pad in an adult after trauma signifies hemarthrosis from an occult intra-articular fracture — radial head most common — until proven otherwise; MRI shows the low-signal line plus marrow edema and separates a true fracture from a bone bruise. The surgical trigger is a mechanical block to forearm rotation, an articular fragment that impedes motion, or comminution (Mason III), NOT the 2 mm number alone (C), so many Mason II fractures without a block are managed nonoperatively. A normal radiograph does not exclude fracture (A). The pseudodefect (D) is a normal posterolateral bare-area groove and does not explain a post-traumatic effusion.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 9-year-old presents after an elbow injury. The radiograph shows an effusion (displaced posterior fat pad) but no clear fracture line, and the ossified trochlea is visible while the medial epicondyle ossification center is not seen. Which conclusion is best supported?

options.text: The medial epicondyle has been avulsed — possibly incarcerated in the joint — because the trochlea never ossifies before the medial epicondyle (CRITOE)
This is Panner disease of the capitellum, which is self-limited and needs only rest
The finding represents primary elbow osteoarthritis, the most common source of loose bodies in this age group
A simple stress reaction of the olecranon apophysis explains the effusion and no further evaluation is needed

moduleQuiz.explanation: By CRITOE (Capitellum, Radial head, Internal/medial epicondyle, Trochlea, Olecranon, External/lateral epicondyle), the trochlea NEVER ossifies before the medial epicondyle. Seeing the trochlea without the medial epicondyle means the epicondyle has been avulsed — classically after a transient dislocation that spontaneously reduced — and it may be incarcerated intra-articularly, a high-stakes pediatric miss. Panner disease (B) affects the capitellum and would not explain a missing medial epicondyle. Primary OA (C) is a non-thrower adult loose-body source, not a pediatric entity. An olecranon stress reaction (D) ignores the CRITOE clue and the need to exclude an incarcerated fragment.

module.title: UCL & Valgus Overload (Thrower)
module.subtitle: The anterior bundle, the T-sign undersurface tear, and the repair-vs-reconstruction conversation in the throwing athlete
topics: UCL anatomy: anterior bundle from medial epicondyle to sublime tubercle as the primary valgus restraint
Normal distal footprint and recess vs the abnormal undercutting threshold (beyond the cartilage edge)
The T-sign: partial undersurface (articular-sided) distal tear and why MR arthrography is most sensitive
Grading and locating: proximal/mid/distal, partial vs full-thickness, acute vs chronic
The medial valgus triad (UCL + flexor-pronator + ulnar nerve) and the repair-vs-reconstruction decision
learningObjectives: Trace the anterior bundle of the UCL from the anteroinferior medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle of the coronoid and explain why it is the primary valgus restraint across the functional throwing arc and the band reconstructed in Tommy John surgery

Recognize the T-sign of a partial undersurface distal tear, distinguish it from a normal distal synovial recess using the articular-cartilage-edge threshold, and state when MR arthrography adds sensitivity

Grade and locate a UCL injury (sprain vs partial undersurface vs full-thickness; proximal/mid/distal; acute vs chronic) and explain why distal and full-thickness tears carry a worse nonoperative prognosis

Read the UCL together with the flexor-pronator origin and the ulnar nerve as the valgus-overload triad and frame the repair-vs-reconstruction conversation by lesion features and athlete demand rather than a single MRI number

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling any sliver of fluid undercutting the distal UCL a tear
commonMistakes.correction: A small synovial recess near the sublime-tubercle insertion is normal; a couple of millimeters of fluid undercutting the most distal fibers can be a normal variant. The abnormal threshold is fluid/contrast tracking distal to the sublime-tubercle margin BEYOND the edge of the articular cartilage (the true T-sign), not 'any undercutting at all.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Teaching distal (sublime-tubercle) UCL tears as the more common pattern in throwers
commonMistakes.correction: Distal-vs-proximal predominance is genuinely not uniform in the literature; several series report proximal/humeral-sided partials as at least as common. Teach distal tears as a recognized, prognostically worse, under-recognized pattern that does worse nonoperatively, NOT as the most frequent lesion. Proximal partials are the ones favored for nonoperative/PRP management.

commonMistakes.mistake: Recommending routine ulnar nerve transposition with every UCL reconstruction, or reporting a partial tear as automatically 'nonoperative' on MRI alone
commonMistakes.correction: Concurrent transposition is selective, done for preoperative ulnar symptoms or instability, not routinely (routine transposition adds morbidity). And management is driven by lesion features plus athlete demand plus the moving-valgus exam, not the MRI grade in isolation; document ulnar neuritis/subluxation and the lesion, then frame the decision rather than dictate it.

topicContent.content:
### The Anterior Bundle: One Band Does the Work

The **ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) complex** has three parts, but for the thrower only one matters. The **anterior bundle** is the **primary valgus restraint** across the **functional throwing arc (~20–120°)**, running from the **anteroinferior medial epicondyle** to the **sublime tubercle of the medial coronoid**. This is the band reconstructed in **Tommy John surgery**.

The other two contribute little to valgus restraint:
- **Posterior bundle** — forms the floor of the **cubital tunnel**; relevant to the ulnar nerve, not to valgus stability.
- **Transverse (Cooper) ligament** — negligible mechanical role.

**How to read it:** the normal anterior bundle is a **thin, taut, uniformly low-signal band** to the sublime tubercle, best on **coronal (or coronal-oblique) fluid-sensitive fat-sat** prescribed *along the band*. The common **flexor-pronator origin** drapes directly over it off the medial epicondyle, so trace the deep low-signal ligament separately from the overlying tendon.

| UCL component | Origin → insertion | Valgus role |
|---|---|---|
| **Anterior bundle** | Anteroinferior medial epicondyle → sublime tubercle | **Primary restraint (the Tommy John band)** |
| Posterior bundle | Medial epicondyle → olecranon (cubital tunnel floor) | Minimal |
| Transverse/Cooper | Coronoid → olecranon | Negligible |

**Note on the available images:** the elbow stacks here are **coronal + axial non-arthrographic T2 fat-sat** — coronal is the plane to read the anterior bundle and the T-sign, with the caveat below that MR arthrography is more sensitive for the undersurface tear.
topicContent.pearl: When a thrower presents with medial pain, the only valgus-relevant ligament is the anterior bundle from the medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle — read it on coronal fluid-sensitive images prescribed along the band, and do not let the overlying flexor-pronator tendon distract you from the deep ligament.

topicContent.content:
### Normal Footprint, Normal Recess, and the Abnormal Threshold

The single most over-called finding here is a **normal distal synovial recess** mistaken for an undersurface tear. The distal footprint normally inserts as a **thin, taut, uniformly low-signal band on the sublime tubercle**, and a **small synovial recess can exist** right at that attachment.

The key is *where the fluid stops*:

| Finding | What you see | Call it |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal recess** | A sliver of fluid undercutting the **most distal fibers by a couple of mm**, NOT past the cartilage edge | Normal variant |
| **Partial undersurface tear (T-sign)** | Fluid/contrast tracking distal to the sublime tubercle **beyond the edge of the articular cartilage** | Abnormal |

The **abnormal threshold is fluid/contrast tracking distal to the sublime-tubercle margin BEYOND the edge of the articular cartilage** — not 'any undercutting at all.' A couple of millimeters undercutting the most distal fibers is within normal limits; the diagnosis turns on the fluid crossing the **articular-cartilage edge**.

**Why it matters:** over-calling a normal recess as a partial tear can push a thrower toward an injection or a surgical conversation he does not need; under-calling a true undersurface tear ends a season. Anchor every distal UCL call to the cartilage-edge landmark, and remember **MR arthrography is more sensitive** than the non-contrast study you are reading.
topicContent.pearl: The abnormal line for the distal UCL is the articular-cartilage edge: a couple of millimeters of fluid undercutting the most distal fibers is a normal recess, but contrast or fluid tracking BEYOND the cartilage margin along the sublime tubercle is the undersurface tear.

topicContent.content:
### The T-Sign: The Classic Throwing Injury

The **T-sign** is the signature of a **partial-thickness undersurface (articular-sided) DISTAL tear** — the classic throwing injury that is routinely **undercalled on non-contrast MRI**.

**What forms the 'T':** on coronal fluid-sensitive MRI or MR arthrography, **fluid/contrast tracks medially along the sublime tubercle UNDER the distal UCL attachment**, beyond the articular-cartilage edge. This medial limb of fluid meets the vertical **joint-fluid column** at a right angle, producing an **inverted / right-angle 'T'** — while the **ligament remains attached more proximally**. The deep fibers are stripped off the bone; the superficial fibers still hold.

| Feature | T-sign (undersurface partial) | Full-thickness tear |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid pattern | Medial limb undercutting distal fibers, beyond cartilage edge | Fluid-bright signal spanning the **entire** thickness |
| Proximal fibers | **Still attached** | Discontinuous / gap / retraction |
| Extravasation | Contained (articular-sided) | **Medial extravasation through the defect** |

**The arthrography caveat:** because the deep surface tear may not fill with native joint fluid on a non-contrast study, **MR arthrography is the most sensitive study** for the T-sign — intra-articular contrast actively undercuts the partial tear. On the non-arthrographic coronal T2 fat-sat available here, you can suggest the T-sign, but if the question is surgically decisive and the study is equivocal, **add MR arthrography**.
topicContent.pearl: The T-sign is fluid undercutting the distal UCL beyond the cartilage edge while the proximal fibers stay attached — it is the under-recognized, season-ending undersurface tear of the throwing elbow, and MR arthrography is the most sensitive study to confirm it when non-contrast imaging is equivocal.

topicContent.content:
### Grade and Locate: The Report That Drives the Decision

The surgeon needs three things from your read: **surface, location, and chronicity.** Give all three explicitly.

**Surface / grade:**
- **Sprain / low-grade** — periligamentous or intrasubstance edema, fibers grossly intact, no surface-reaching defect, no T-sign.
- **Partial undersurface tear** — the T-sign; articular-sided, proximal fibers attached.
- **Full-thickness** — **fluid-bright signal spanning the entire thickness with a gap/retraction, or medial extravasation through the defect.**

A **<50% / >50% partial-thickness call is a useful descriptive heuristic, not a formally validated or named UCL grading scale.** Do not imply a validated cutoff drives the decision by itself.

**Location:** name it — **proximal (humeral) / midsubstance / distal (sublime tubercle).** This is not cosmetic: **distal (sublime-tubercle) tears are clinically important, more often occult, and do worse nonoperatively**, which is exactly why naming the location matters. But distal-vs-proximal **predominance is genuinely not uniform in the literature** — teach the distal tear as a recognized, prognostically worse, under-recognized pattern, **not** as the more frequent lesion. **Proximal partials** are the ones favored for nonoperative/PRP management.

**Chronicity:** a **thickened, heterogeneous, or ossified** ligament reads as **chronic**; clean acute fiber disruption reads as acute. The repair-vs-reconstruction conversation cares about **avulsion off bone (good tissue) vs a shredded chronic midsubstance.**

| Axis | Acute / favorable | Chronic / unfavorable |
|---|---|---|
| Surface | Clean partial or avulsion | Diffuse attenuation |
| Location | Proximal (better nonop/PRP) | Distal (worse nonop) |
| Tissue | Avulsion off bone | Thickened/heterogeneous/ossified |
topicContent.pearl: Always report surface, location, and chronicity: a <50%/>50% split is a descriptive heuristic and not a validated scale, distal/full-thickness tears do worse nonoperatively, and an 'avulsion off bone vs shredded midsubstance' distinction is exactly what the surgeon needs to choose repair over reconstruction.

topicContent.content:
### The Medial Triad and the Repair-vs-Reconstruction Conversation

Medial-sided valgus overload **travels as a triad** — read all three on every thrower:

| Component | What to document |
|---|---|
| **UCL anterior bundle** | Surface, location, chronicity (above) |
| **Flexor-pronator (common flexor) origin** | Strain vs tendinosis off the medial epicondyle, overlying the UCL |
| **Ulnar nerve** | Neuritis (caliber + T2 signal) and **flexion subluxation** in the cubital tunnel |

A common incidental bony clue to chronic valgus overload is the **sublime-tubercle traction spur / ulnar traction marrow change**.

**Management is driven by the lesion PLUS the athlete's demand, not the MRI alone:**
- **Most partial/low-grade tears in throwers are managed nonoperatively first** — rest, flexor-pronator and kinetic-chain rehab, structured return-to-throwing; **PRP is increasingly used, especially for proximal partials.**
- **Reconstruction (Tommy John, autograft)** is the conversation for **full-thickness tears in throwers wanting high-level return**, **high-grade/symptomatic partials that fail ~3–6 months of rehab**, or **valgus instability discordant with demand.**
- **Repair ± internal brace** has re-emerged for **acute avulsion-type tears with good tissue**, particularly younger/lower-demand or earlier-return athletes — which is why the **avulsion-vs-shredded-midsubstance** distinction matters.

**Two hedges to honor:** the failed-conservative interval, the repair-vs-reconstruction boundary, and PRP efficacy are all **evolving — present decision DRIVERS, not a rigid cutoff.** And **ulnar nerve transposition with reconstruction is selective**, done for preoperative ulnar symptoms or instability, not routinely.

**The number MRI cannot give:** **dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound** quantifies side-to-side **medial ulnohumeral gapping** — a side-to-side difference of roughly **>1 mm (commonly cited ~1.5–2 mm)** is the functional instability threshold. For counseling, typical return is **~12–15 months after reconstruction vs ~6–9 months after repair + internal brace**, and prevention rests on **pitch-count/workload limits and avoiding breaking balls before skeletal maturity.**
topicContent.pearl: Read the UCL, the flexor-pronator origin, and the ulnar nerve as one valgus triad, then frame management by lesion plus demand — nonoperative/PRP for most partials, reconstruction for full-thickness or rehab-failed high-demand throwers, and repair ± internal brace for acute avulsions with good tissue — while remembering that dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound, not MRI, supplies the side-to-side gapping number.

moduleQuiz.stem: A 19-year-old collegiate pitcher has medial elbow pain. You are setting up to evaluate the band that is the primary valgus restraint and the structure reconstructed in Tommy John surgery. Which structure and course should you trace?

options.text: The posterior bundle of the UCL, forming the floor of the cubital tunnel
The transverse (Cooper) ligament, running from the coronoid to the olecranon
The anterior bundle of the UCL, from the anteroinferior medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle of the coronoid
The common flexor-pronator tendon, from the medial epicondyle to the proximal forearm flexors

moduleQuiz.explanation: The anterior bundle of the UCL is the primary valgus restraint across the functional throwing arc (~20–120°), running from the anteroinferior medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle of the medial coronoid, and it is the band reconstructed in Tommy John surgery. The posterior bundle (cubital tunnel floor) and the transverse/Cooper ligament contribute little to valgus restraint. The flexor-pronator tendon is a muscle-tendon origin that overlies the UCL but is not the valgus-restraining ligament; it is read as the second leg of the medial triad, not as the reconstructed band.

moduleQuiz.stem: On a coronal fluid-sensitive image of a thrower's elbow, you see fluid tracking medially along the sublime tubercle, undercutting the distal UCL attachment beyond the edge of the articular cartilage and meeting the joint-fluid column at a right angle, while the more proximal ligament fibers remain attached. What does this represent?

options.text: A partial-thickness undersurface (articular-sided) distal tear — the T-sign
A normal distal synovial recess that requires no further comment
A full-thickness UCL tear with retraction
Flexor-pronator tendinosis overlying an intact UCL

moduleQuiz.explanation: The described pattern is the T-sign: fluid/contrast undercutting the distal/sublime-tubercle attachment BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge, forming a right-angle 'T' with the joint-fluid column while the proximal fibers stay attached. That is the signature of a partial-thickness undersurface (articular-sided) distal tear — the classic, frequently undercalled throwing injury. A normal recess undercuts only the most distal fibers by a couple of millimeters and does NOT cross the cartilage edge, which is the key discriminator. A full-thickness tear would show fluid-bright signal spanning the entire thickness with a gap, retraction, or medial extravasation, not preserved proximal fibers. MR arthrography is the most sensitive study to confirm the T-sign when non-contrast MRI is equivocal.

moduleQuiz.stem: An 18-year-old high-demand pitcher has a symptomatic partial UCL tear that has not improved after a structured rehab and return-to-throwing program. He is considering surgery. Which statement best reflects how the repair-vs-reconstruction decision and adjunctive findings should be framed?

options.text: A <50% vs >50% partial-thickness measurement is a validated grading scale that, by itself, dictates whether to reconstruct
Ulnar nerve transposition should be performed routinely with every UCL reconstruction to prevent later neuritis
Distal sublime-tubercle tears are the most common pattern in throwers and respond best to nonoperative management
The decision is driven by lesion features plus athlete demand and the moving-valgus exam, with dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound supplying a side-to-side gapping number MRI cannot give

moduleQuiz.explanation: Management is driven by the lesion plus the athlete's demand and the moving-valgus exam, not by the MRI grade in isolation; reconstruction is the conversation for full-thickness tears in high-demand throwers, high-grade or symptomatic partials that fail roughly 3–6 months of rehab, or instability discordant with demand, while repair ± internal brace has re-emerged for acute avulsion-type tears with good tissue. The <50%/>50% split is a descriptive heuristic, not a validated named scale (A is wrong). Ulnar nerve transposition is selective — done for preoperative ulnar symptoms or instability, not routinely, because routine transposition adds morbidity (B is wrong). Distal tears do worse nonoperatively and their predominance is not uniform in the literature, so calling them both the most common and the best nonoperative responders is wrong (C). Dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound quantifies side-to-side medial gapping (commonly cited ~1.5–2 mm), the functional threshold static MRI cannot provide.

module.title: LCL Complex, PLRI & Post-Traumatic Instability
module.subtitle: The LUCL and posterolateral rotatory instability, the coronoid/anteromedial-facet axis and VPMRI, the terrible triad, Osborne-Cotterill, and heterotopic ossification
topics: LCL complex anatomy and the LUCL as the principal restraint to PLRI
PLRI mechanism and the deep undersurface tear hiding beneath refractory 'tennis elbow'
O'Driscoll circle of dislocation, LCL grading vocabulary, and secondary PLRI signs
Anteromedial coronoid facet fracture and varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI)
Terrible triad, the Osborne-Cotterill lesion, heterotopic ossification, and the management hinge
learningObjectives: Identify the components of the LCL complex and explain why the lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL) is the key restraint to posterolateral rotatory instability, tracing it on coronal AND axial images
Recognize the LCL injury patterns of dislocation and the iatrogenic post-lateral-release LUCL tear, and separate a symptomatic deep LUCL/RCL tear from accompanying common-extensor tendinosis
Operationalize the coronoid/anteromedial-facet axis and varus posteromedial rotatory instability, and apply the Regan-Morrey (height) and O'Driscoll (anteromedial facet) coronoid classifications
Recognize the terrible triad, the Osborne-Cotterill lesion, and post-traumatic heterotopic ossification, and route each instability pattern to the correct disposition

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling lateral epicondylitis and stopping, without tracing the LUCL to the supinator crest
commonMistakes.correction: The LUCL/RCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon, so a deep undersurface tear that undercuts or communicates with the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies PLRI — the lesion that converts refractory 'tennis elbow' into a surgical instability problem and a classic complication of prior lateral steroid injection or release. Conversely, do not over-call reactive lateral-epicondyle marrow edema or a thin reactive RCL signal as an LCL tear.

commonMistakes.mistake: Dismissing a small anteromedial coronoid facet fragment as stable because it looks small on radiographs
commonMistakes.correction: The anteromedial facet fragment is small on radiographs but highly unstable — it implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI), a distinct mechanism from PLRI, and untreated drives early arthrosis. It is surgical. Classify it with the O'Driscoll anteromedial-facet system (use Regan-Morrey only for coronoid height), and CT maps the osseous fragment best.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming the medial (ulnar) collateral ligament is the primary lesion in a simple elbow dislocation
commonMistakes.correction: By the O'Driscoll circle, soft-tissue disruption proceeds lateral-to-medial — LCL/LUCL first, then anterior and posterior capsule, and the MCL last — so the LCL is the primary lesion in most simple dislocations and valgus stability is often preserved. Comment explicitly on LUCL integrity in every post-dislocation and post-lateral-surgery elbow.

topicContent.content:
### The LCL Complex and the LUCL as the PLRI Restraint

The **lateral collateral ligament (LCL) complex** is read on coronal and axial fluid-sensitive fat-sat images. It has four named components:

| Component | Course | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Radial collateral ligament (RCL)** | Lateral epicondyle → blends into the **annular ligament** | Lateral stabilizer; deep to the common extensor |
| **Lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL)** | Lateral epicondyle → arcs **posterior to the radial head** → **supinator crest** of the ulna | **Principal restraint to PLRI** |
| **Annular ligament** | Encircles the radial neck (best on **axial**) | Stabilizes the radial head |
| **Accessory LCL** | Supinator crest to annular ligament | Supports the annular ligament |

The **LUCL is the structure that matters most** here. It originates at the lateral epicondyle, sweeps **posterior to the radial head**, and inserts on the **supinator crest** — and it is the principal restraint to **posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI)**.

**Trace its posterolateral arc across consecutive coronal AND axial images.** Partial-volume averaging is the single most common reason the LUCL is missed — it is an oblique, curving structure, and one plane is not enough. A normal LUCL is a thin, continuous, uniformly low-signal band to the supinator crest.

**The anatomic trap:** the **LUCL/RCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon**. A deep undersurface tear there hides beneath what looks like simple lateral epicondylitis — which is exactly why the next topic exists.
topicContent.pearl: The LUCL runs lateral epicondyle → posterior to the radial head → supinator crest and is the key PLRI restraint — trace it on BOTH coronal and axial images, because partial-volume averaging on a single plane is the main reason it is missed.

topicContent.content:
### PLRI and the Deep Tear Hiding Beneath Refractory 'Tennis Elbow'

**Posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI)** is **LUCL incompetence** that allows posterolateral subluxation of the radius and ulna off the capitellum — the lesion behind the clinical **lateral pivot-shift apprehension**. It is the **most common chronic/recurrent elbow instability** and follows:

- **Dislocation or subluxation** (the LUCL is usually the primary lesion — see the O'Driscoll circle)
- **Iatrogenic injury** from an **aggressive lateral epicondylitis release** or **radial-head surgery**

**Why this is a satisfaction-of-search trap:** the **LUCL/RCL origin sits deep to the common extensor tendon**. A deep undersurface tear at that origin can hide beneath what reads as routine lateral epicondylitis. This is **the lesion that converts refractory 'tennis elbow' into instability** — and it is a classic complication of prior **lateral corticosteroid injection or surgical release**.

**What separates the surgical lesion from over-call:**

| Finding | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Thickened, T2-bright common extensor, **intact deep fibers** | Tendinosis / partial CET tear → conservative |
| **Fluid-bright gap** at the origin that **undercuts/communicates with the radiocapitellar joint AND involves the LUCL** | Deep tear implying **PLRI → surgical** |
| Reactive lateral-epicondyle marrow edema, **thin RCL signal alone** | **Normal/reactive — do NOT call an LCL tear** |

**Always trace the LUCL to the supinator crest before signing off any high-grade lateral epicondylitis.** The companion ligament is where the surgery hides.
topicContent.pearl: A deep common-extensor tear with a fluid-bright gap that undercuts the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL is PLRI, not tennis elbow — it is a known complication of prior lateral injection/release and turns a debridement into a ligament reconstruction.

topicContent.content:
### O'Driscoll Circle, LCL Grading Vocabulary, and Secondary PLRI Signs

**The O'Driscoll circle of dislocation** explains the sequence of soft-tissue failure: disruption proceeds **lateral-to-medial** — **LCL/LUCL first → anterior and posterior capsule → finally the MCL.** Two consequences follow directly:

1. The **LCL is the primary lesion in most simple dislocations.**
2. Valgus stability is often **preserved**, because the MCL fails last.

So: **comment explicitly on LUCL integrity in every post-dislocation and post-lateral-surgery elbow.**

**Grading vocabulary** mirrors the UCL — there is **no standardized single MRI measurement** for an LUCL tear, so call it on **continuity, surface, and radiocapitellar alignment** plus the clinical pivot-shift, not a millimeter cutoff:

| Grade | Appearance |
|---|---|
| **Intact** | Thin, continuous, uniformly low-signal band to the supinator crest |
| **Sprain** | Thickening/edema, **fibers intact** |
| **Partial** | Surface-reaching fluid **not spanning** the full thickness |
| **Full-thickness** | Discontinuity/avulsion, usually **proximal/epicondylar** |

**Secondary PLRI signs** (look for these when the ligament itself is equivocal):
- **Posterolateral radial subluxation**
- **Radiocapitellar incongruity**
- **Lateral capsular and deep common-extensor tear**

Because there is no number to lean on, the **combination** of a discontinuous LUCL, radiocapitellar malalignment, and the clinical pivot-shift is what makes the call.
topicContent.pearl: Dislocation tears the elbow lateral-to-medial (O'Driscoll: LUCL → capsule → MCL last), so the LCL is the primary lesion in most simple dislocations and valgus is often spared — comment on LUCL integrity in every post-dislocation elbow.

topicContent.content:
### Anteromedial Coronoid Facet Fracture and VPMRI

**Varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI)** is the **third, distinct instability mechanism** — separate from valgus instability and from PLRI. Its imaging signature is an **anteromedial coronoid facet fracture + LUCL injury**.

The critical teaching point: the **anteromedial facet fragment looks small on radiographs but is highly unstable and surgical.** Untreated VPMRI drives **early arthrosis**. This is a classic **don't-miss** MRI/CT call — **small does not mean stable at the coronoid.**

**Two coronoid classifications — use them for different things:**

| System | Classifies | Scheme |
|---|---|---|
| **O'Driscoll** | The **anteromedial facet** (the VPMRI lesion) | Anteromedial-facet subtypes |
| **Regan-Morrey** | Coronoid **height** | I = tip avulsion · II = ≤50% · III = >50% of coronoid height |
